bibliographic_data.title: Continual Learning of Recurrent Neural Networks by Locally Aligning Distributed Representations
bibliographic_data.abstract: Temporal models based on recurrent neural networks have proven to be quite powerful in a wide variety of applications, including language modeling and speech processing. However, training these models often relies on back-propagation through time, which entails unfolding the network over many time steps, making the process of conducting credit assignment considerably more challenging. Furthermore, the nature of back-propagation itself does not permit the use of non-differentiable activation functions and is inherently sequential, making parallelization of the underlying training process difficult. Here, we propose the Parallel Temporal Neural Coding Network (P-TNCN), a biologically inspired model trained by the learning algorithm we call Local Representation Alignment. It aims to resolve the difficulties and problems that plague recurrent networks trained by back-propagation through time. The architecture requires neither unrolling in time nor the derivatives of its internal activation functions. We compare our model and learning procedure to other back-propagation through time alternatives (which also tend to be computationally expensive), including real-time recurrent learning, echo state networks, and unbiased online recurrent optimization. We show that it outperforms these on sequence modeling benchmarks such as Bouncing MNIST, a new benchmark we denote as Bouncing NotMNIST, and Penn Treebank. Notably, our approach can in some instances outperform full back-propagation through time as well as variants such as sparse attentive back-tracking.

core_content.novelty_claim: The P-TNCN architecture allows for the parallelization of training and inference, using a learning algorithm called Local Representation Alignment that can handle non-differentiable activation functions.
core_content.key_methodology: The P-TNCN uses error units to allow parallelization at both training and test time. The learning algorithm generalizes a recently proposed local learning algorithm known as Local Representation Alignment (LRA).
outcomes: Outperforms competing online alternatives on sequence modeling benchmarks.
Can adapt to new datasets even if synaptic weights are fixed (zero-shot adaptation).
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper introduces the Parallel Temporal Neural Coding Network (P-TNCN), a model that addresses the limitations of back-propagation through time in training recurrent neural networks. The P-TNCN is designed to resolve difficulties such as long-term dependency capture, non-differentiable activation functions, and sequential nature of backpropagation. It uses Local Representation Alignment (LRA) for learning, which allows parallelization at both training and test times. The model outperforms other online alternatives on benchmarks like Bouncing MNIST and Penn Treebank.
